instructions: Sit comfortably with the spine erect. Rest the left hand on the left knee.
Raise the right hand. Fold the index and middle fingers to the palm, leaving the thumb, ring, and little fingers extended (Vishnu Mudra).
Close the right nostril with the thumb. Inhale slowly through the left nostril for 4 counts.
Close both nostrils. Pause briefly at the top if comfortable.
Open the right nostril and exhale fully through it for 4 counts.
Inhale through the right nostril for 4 counts.
Close both nostrils at the top, then open the left and exhale fully for 4 counts. This is one complete cycle.
Continue for 5–10 cycles, keeping the breath smooth, silent, and effortless.
benefits: Balances both hemispheres of the brain
Calms anxiety, mental chatter, and stress
Removes energetic blockages in the nadis
Excellent preparation for meditation

instructions: Sit or lie comfortably with the spine long and the jaw relaxed.
Slightly constrict the back of the throat — as if fogging a mirror with your mouth closed.
Inhale through the nose with this constriction. You should hear a soft, oceanic sound.
Exhale through the nose with the same gentle throat constriction, producing the same sound.
Let the breath be slow, smooth, and continuous — no pausing between inhale and exhale.
Maintain a 1:1 ratio: equal length inhale and exhale.
Use Ujjayi as the base breath throughout your asana practice, matching breath to movement.
benefits: Builds heat and internal focus during asana practice
Calms the nervous system and reduces stress
Strengthens respiratory muscles
Creates a steady, meditative rhythm for the practice

instructions: Sit with the spine erect. Place hands on knees. Take a deep, passive inhale through the nose.
Begin sharp, forceful exhalations: sharply contract the lower belly inward toward the spine. The inhale that follows is completely passive and automatic.
Find a pumping rhythm — approximately one forceful exhale per second.
Complete one round of 30–50 pumps, then take a full inhale, hold briefly, and exhale slowly.
Rest for one normal breath cycle, notice the effects, then begin the next round.
Start with 2 rounds of 30 pumps. Gradually increase to 3 rounds of 60–100 pumps over weeks.
Stop immediately if you feel lightheaded. Never force retention.
benefits: Detoxifies the lungs and respiratory tract
Stimulates Agni (digestive fire) and metabolism
Clears mental fog and energises the body
Strengthens the abdominal muscles

instructions: Sit comfortably with the spine erect. Relax the shoulders.
Unlike Kapalabhati, BOTH the inhale and exhale are equally forceful — like a blacksmith's bellows.
Inhale powerfully and fully through the nose, expanding the chest. Exhale powerfully and fully through the nose, contracting the belly.
Maintain a pace of approximately one full cycle (in + out) per second.
After 10–15 cycles, take a full deep inhale, hold for 5–10 seconds, then exhale slowly.
Rest and breathe normally for one full minute before the next round.
Limit to 2–3 rounds. Stop immediately if you feel dizzy or anxious.
benefits: Rapidly increases prana and oxygen in the blood
Clears Kapha sluggishness and stagnation
Builds intense internal heat
Prepares the system for deep pranayama and meditation

instructions: Sit comfortably with the spine erect.
Roll the tongue lengthwise into a tube shape and extend it slightly beyond the lips. (If you cannot curl your tongue, use Sitkari / Hissing Breath instead.)
Inhale slowly and deeply through the tube-shaped tongue, drawing cool air over its surface.
At the top of the inhale, draw the tongue in and close the mouth.
Exhale slowly and completely through both nostrils.
This is one cycle. Continue for 8–12 cycles.
Notice the cooling, calming sensation as the air contacts the moist surface of the tongue.
benefits: Cools excess Pitta and reduces body temperature
Calms anger, irritability, and impatience
Soothes the digestive system and reduces acidity
Excellent for hot climates or after vigorous practice

instructions: Sit comfortably with the spine erect.
Press the upper and lower teeth together lightly and part the lips to expose the teeth.
Inhale slowly through the teeth — you will hear a soft hissing sound as cool air passes over the teeth and tongue.
At the end of the inhale, close the mouth and exhale slowly and fully through both nostrils.
Continue for 8–12 cycles.
This technique is the recommended alternative to Sitali for those who cannot curl the tongue.
Notice the cooling, calming effect building with each cycle.
benefits: Cools the body and reduces excess heat
Calms inflammation and nervous irritation
Reduces excess Pitta without requiring tongue-rolling
Useful before sleep or in hot weather

instructions: Sit comfortably with the spine erect. Close the eyes and soften the face.
Place the index fingers lightly over the ear flaps (tragus) and press gently to block external sound.
Inhale deeply and slowly through the nose.
Exhale slowly through the nose while making a deep, steady humming sound — like the sound of a bee. Keep the mouth closed throughout.
The vibration should be felt in the skull, sinuses, and face.
Keep the exhale as smooth and long as possible.
This is one round. Continue for 5–10 rounds.
After the last round, sit quietly and observe the stillness and vibration in the mind.
benefits: Instantly calms anxiety, stress, and mental agitation
Relieves tension headaches and migraines
Soothes the nervous system through vibrational resonance
Highly effective for insomnia — practice just before sleep

instructions: Sit comfortably with the spine erect. Begin with natural breath awareness for one minute.
Inhale through the nose for a count of 4.
Hold the breath with full lungs for a count of 4.
Exhale through the nose for a count of 4.
Hold with empty lungs for a count of 4.
This is one complete box cycle. Repeat for 5–10 cycles.
As you become comfortable, extend each phase to a count of 5, then 6.
Never strain — the count is a guide, not a goal. Keep the transitions smooth.
benefits: Rapidly regulates the autonomic nervous system
Builds concentration and mental clarity
Reduces stress and activates the relaxation response
Excellent preparation for meditation or high-pressure situations

instructions: Sit comfortably with the spine erect.
Choose a ratio. A common starting ratio is 1:2 — inhale for 4 counts, exhale for 8 counts.
Inhale slowly through the nose for 4 counts.
Exhale slowly through the nose for 8 counts — allow the breath to become finer and finer toward the end.
The prolonged exhale is what activates the parasympathetic nervous system.
Continue for 8–12 cycles.
Advanced ratios include 1:2:4 (inhale:hold:exhale). Only progress when the current ratio is completely effortless.
benefits: Activates the parasympathetic nervous system through extended exhalation
Deepens relaxation and reduces resting heart rate
Develops breath control and respiratory endurance
Calms mental restlessness without inducing drowsiness

instructions: Sit comfortably with the spine erect. Use Vishnu Mudra (right hand, index and middle fingers folded).
Close the left nostril with the ring finger.
Inhale slowly and fully through the right nostril.
At the top of the inhale, close both nostrils briefly (optional retention).
Release the right nostril. Close the right with the thumb. Exhale slowly and fully through the left nostril.
Immediately close the left nostril and inhale through the right again.
Always inhale through the right, exhale through the left — this is the defining rule of Surya Bhedana.
Continue for 8–12 cycles. Avoid in the evening, if you have high blood pressure, or in hot weather.
benefits: Activates the solar (Pingala) channel, increasing energy
Stimulates metabolism and digestive fire (Agni)
Counteracts Kapha sluggishness in the morning
Warms the body in cold weather

instructions: Sit comfortably with the spine erect. Use Vishnu Mudra (right hand, index and middle fingers folded).
Close the right nostril with the right thumb.
Inhale slowly and fully through the left nostril.
At the top of the inhale, close both nostrils briefly (optional retention).
Release the left nostril. Close the left with the ring finger. Exhale slowly and fully through the right nostril.
Immediately close the right nostril and inhale through the left again.
Always inhale through the left, exhale through the right — the opposite of Surya Bhedana.
Continue for 8–12 cycles. Particularly excellent as an evening or pre-sleep practice.
benefits: Activates the lunar (Ida) channel, inducing calm
Cools excess Pitta, reduces heat and irritability
Promotes introspection and prepares for sleep
Balances the nervous system after stressful activity

instructions: Sit comfortably with the spine erect. Use Vishnu Mudra with the right hand.
Inhale normally and fully through both nostrils.
Close the right nostril with the thumb. Exhale slowly and completely through the left nostril.
Inhale again through both nostrils fully.
Close the left nostril with the ring finger. Exhale slowly and completely through the right nostril.
This is one cycle: inhale through both, alternate the exhale nostril.
Continue for 8–12 cycles, maintaining a smooth, even rhythm.
The key feature is that the inhale is always bilateral (both nostrils) — only the exhale alternates.
benefits: Gently balances the nadis without the complexity of full alternate nostril breathing
Promotes mental equilibrium and emotional steadiness
Calming yet clarifying — suitable any time of day
Good introductory technique before progressing to Nadi Shodhana

instructions: Sit comfortably or lie down with the spine long.
Viloma 1 (Interrupted Inhale): Inhale for 2 counts, pause. Inhale for 2 more counts, pause. Inhale for 2 more counts to fill the lungs fully. Exhale smoothly and continuously.
Viloma 2 (Interrupted Exhale): Inhale fully and smoothly. Exhale for 2 counts, pause. Exhale 2 more counts, pause. Exhale 2 more counts to empty fully.
The pauses are gentle, comfortable holds — not forced breath-stops. The breath should feel like climbing or descending stairs.
Choose either Viloma 1 or Viloma 2 per session — do not mix.
Continue for 5–10 cycles.
Viloma 1 is slightly more energising; Viloma 2 is more calming.
benefits: Increases lung capacity and breath control
Develops fine awareness of the breath
Calming for Vata anxiety and mental restlessness
Excellent preparation for retention practices

instructions: Lie down or sit comfortably with the spine long. Place one hand on the belly and one on the chest.
Part 1 — Belly: Inhale slowly into the belly first. Let it rise like a balloon filling with air.
Part 2 — Ribcage: Continue the same inhale into the ribcage. Feel the ribs expand sideways, like an accordion opening.
Part 3 — Upper Chest: Finally, let the breath rise into the upper chest — the collarbones lift gently.
Exhale in reverse: upper chest softens first, then ribcage draws in, then belly falls.
Each cycle should be slow, smooth, and completely without force.
Continue for 8–12 cycles, feeling the breath capacity deepen naturally with each cycle.
benefits: Maximises lung capacity with each breath cycle
Grounds excess Vata — deeply calming and stabilising
Restores natural full-body breathing lost to shallow breathing habits
Excellent for beginners as the foundation for all pranayama

instructions: Sit in a comfortable meditation posture with the spine erect. This is an advanced technique — only practice under guidance.
Inhale slowly and deeply through both nostrils.
At the top of the inhale, apply Jalandhara Bandha (chin lock): gently lower the chin toward the sternum, lengthening the back of the neck. The spine remains erect.
Retain the breath comfortably with the lock engaged for 5–10 seconds.
Slowly release the chin lock and raise the head.
Exhale very gradually through the nose — the exhale should take twice as long as the inhale.
After the exhale, pause for a moment in stillness before the next inhale.
Stop immediately if you feel any discomfort, pressure, or dizziness.
benefits: Induces deep pratyahara (withdrawal of the senses)
Turns awareness profoundly inward — preparatory for meditation
Calms excessive mental activity and emotional fluctuation
Strengthens the throat and neck through the bandha application

instructions: Sit comfortably in a meditation posture. This technique is primarily meditative.
Inhale deeply and slowly through the nose — as you inhale, consciously direct attention to the sensation of expansion, lightness, and buoyancy in the torso.
At the top of the inhale, hold gently. Imagine the body becoming lighter, almost as if it could float on water.
Exhale very slowly through the nose, maintaining the sense of lightness and subtle expansion throughout.
Do not let the exhale collapse into heaviness — carry the quality of lightness through the entire cycle.
Continue for 5–8 cycles, remaining in a calm, inward, meditative state.
This technique is more about the quality of awareness than the mechanics of the breath.
benefits: Cultivates lightness, ease, and meditative awareness
Subtle energetic cleansing of the respiratory system
Deepens the felt sense of prana in the body
Bridges pranayama and deep meditation

instructions: Sit comfortably with the spine erect. Begin with 5 minutes of natural breath awareness.
Inhale fully through the nose for 4 counts.
Antara Kumbhaka (internal retention): Hold the breath at the top for 4–16 counts, depending on your level. Keep the face and throat relaxed.
Exhale slowly through the nose for 8 counts.
Optionally: Bahya Kumbhaka (external retention): Hold with empty lungs for 2–4 counts before the next inhale.
The ratio for practice is typically 1:4:2 (inhale:hold:exhale). Begin with 1:1:2 and build gradually over weeks.
Never strain during retention. Build duration over months, not days.
Do not practice Kumbhaka if you have heart conditions, hypertension, or are pregnant.
benefits: Builds prana retention and vitality
Deepens meditation by suspending mental activity
Strengthens the diaphragm and respiratory muscles
Energises and revitalises the entire system when practiced correctly

instructions: Sit comfortably with the spine erect.
Curl the tongue into a tube shape and extend it slightly beyond the lips. (Use parted teeth, Sitkari-style, if you cannot curl the tongue.)
Inhale deeply and slowly through the tongue tube for 6–8 counts, drawing cool air over its surface.
At the top of the inhale, apply Jalandhara Bandha (chin lock) and hold the breath for 4–6 counts.
Release the chin lock, close the mouth, and exhale slowly through both nostrils for 8–10 counts.
The extended retention and longer exhale distinguish this from basic Sitali.
Continue for 6–10 cycles.
Particularly effective during summer, or when Pitta symptoms (heat, irritability, inflammation) are elevated.
benefits: Deep cooling for Pitta excess and heat-related conditions
Supports liver and adrenal function
Combines the cooling of Sitali with the stillness of Kumbhaka
Excellent for reducing inflammation and emotional heat

instructions: Practice only on an empty stomach — minimum 4 hours after a meal.
Stand with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent. Place the hands on the thighs just above the knees.
Inhale deeply, then exhale fully and hold the breath out (Bahya Kumbhaka — external retention).
While holding the breath out, rapidly pump the abdominal muscles: sharply draw the belly in toward the spine, then release it outward. This is one pump.
Perform 5–10 pumps per retention. Keep the rest of the body still.
When you need to breathe, stop the pumping, then inhale slowly.
Rest for one normal breath before the next round.
Build gradually: begin with 2 rounds of 5 pumps and progress to 3 rounds of 20–30 pumps over several weeks.
benefits: Directly stimulates Agni (digestive fire) and improves digestion
Massages the abdominal organs and intestines
Detoxifies the lower abdomen and removes Kapha stagnation
Strengthens the core and improves elimination

instructions: Sit in a comfortable meditation posture with the spine erect.
Take a slow, deep inhale through the nose.

At the top of the inhale, engage Mula Bandha: a gentle contraction of the perineum and pelvic floor — similar to the sensation of stopping the flow of urine. The contraction should be subtle and internal, not forceful.
Hold the breath and the lock for 3–5 counts.
Slowly exhale through the nose, gradually releasing the lock as the breath flows out.
Allow the pelvic floor to fully relax before the next inhale — this release is as important as the contraction.
The lock should feel like a gentle inward lift. Tightening the face, holding the breath with strain, or squeezing the buttocks means the bandha is too forceful.
Practice 8–12 cycles. Build gradually — the lock should strengthen subtly over weeks.
benefits: Grounds excess Vata energy in the lower body
Strengthens the pelvic floor and supports the lower spine
Builds energy conservation and reduces energetic dissipation
Prepares the system for advanced pranayama and meditation
